mnemonics.title: Dijkstra's Greedy Steps
mnemonics.content: PQ: Pop vertex with smallest distance, Update neighbors if shorter path found. Remember: Non-negative weights are MUST for correctness.

mnemonics.title: Why Negative Fails
mnemonics.content: NEGATIVE: Never Expect Valid Results On Negative Edges - Because shorter path may arrive later through undiscovered negative edge.

mnemonics.title: Complexity Components
mnemonics.content: EXTRACT V times × log V + RELAX E times × log V = (V+E) log V

acronyms.usage: The tree formed by predecessor pointers containing shortest paths from source to all vertices
Special case of Dijkstra with all edge weights equal to 1
All-pairs shortest path algorithm using dynamic programming, handles negative weights

visualTips.visualization: Think of updating distance as 'stretching' the shortest path wire. If going through vertex u gives a shorter 'wire' to v, we 're-route' the connection.
Imagine a pool of islands connected by bridges with toll costs. The algorithm always picks the cheapest-to-reach island first, locks in that cost, then explores from there.
Like a leaderboard that constantly updates as better scores (shorter distances) are found. The min-heap ensures we always process the current best candidate.

analogies.analogy: Like finding the cheapest flight: always pick the currently cheapest next destination. Once booked (extracted), you know that's your best option because all remaining flights cost at least as much.
If someone could owe you money (negative weight), a path discovered later might actually be cheaper than the one you thought was cheapest - breaking the greedy guarantee.
Dijkstra is like asking locals for directions as you go (greedy, one look ahead). Bellman-Ford is like checking every possible route repeatedly (exhaustive, dynamic programming).
